title: Treasury Market Liquidity Since April 2025

source.name: New York Fed Liberty Street Economics

summary: The New York Fed staff analyze Treasury market liquidity over the past year (April 2025 through February 2026), finding that liquidity worsened markedly following the April 2, 2025 tariff announcement but recovered quickly after the April 9 partial rollback of new tariffs. By early 2026, all three liquidity metrics (bid-ask spreads, order book depth, and price impact) had returned to levels comparable to or better than pre-tariff periods, with volatility trending down despite subsequent policy developments including reciprocal tariffs and Supreme Court rulings on emergency tariffs.
key_points: Volatility spiked sharply after the April 2, 2025 tariff announcement, peaking April 7-9, with the 10-year yield initially declining to ~3.9% before rising ~60bps to 4.5% by April 8
Bid-ask spreads widened markedly after April 2 but less than during March 2020 COVID crisis or March 2023 regional banking turmoil
Order book depth declined to lowest levels since March 2023 in April 2025 but recovered to pre-tarffic levels by late summer 2025
Price impact (per $100M in net order flow) reverted quickly after the April spike and reached the lowest levels since 2021 by early 2026
Liquidity remained stable through subsequent events including reciprocal tariff implementation, Supreme Court tariff decision (Feb 20), and new temporary tariffs
Analysis uses three measures from interdealer BrokerTec data: bid-ask spread, order book depth, and price impact for on-the-run 2-, 5-, and 10-year notes
why_it_matters: Treasury market liquidity is critical for monetary policy transmission, government financing, and financial risk management. This analysis quantifies the market's resilience to policy shocks and provides empirical benchmarks for normal liquidity conditions, helping policymakers and market participants assess current conditions against historical episodes like COVID-2020 and the 2023 banking stress.
macro_context: While this is a market structure analysis, it documents the Treasury market's reaction to macro catalysts including tariff announcements, showing how political/economic uncertainty propagates through volatility and liquidity channels. The analysis ends February 27, 2026, before the Iran conflict referenced in the article.
market_structure_context: The post provides detailed interdealer market structure analysis using three liquidity metrics (bid-ask spreads, order book depth, price impact) calculated from BrokerTec high-frequency data. It compares the April 2025 episode to prior stress periods (March 2020, March 2023) and tracks recovery through February 2026.
important_numbers: $30 trillion in marketable Treasury debt outstanding as of Feb 28

issues_found: Minor typo in key_points: 'pre-tarffic levels' should be 'pre-tariff levels'

Order book depth claim slightly understates the source: source says 'similar to, if not better than, any time since March 2022' but record says 'recovered to pre-tariff levels' (implies only pre-April 2025)

human_review.notes: These are minor editorial issues that do not affect factual accuracy of the core findings. The record is well-constructed from an authoritative source.

notes: Authors Henry Dyer and Michael J. Fleming are NY Fed staff. Article ends February 27, 2026 data; authors note the analysis does not reflect effects from the conflict with Iran that began March 1, 2026. The 10-year yield decline and subsequent rise following the April 2 announcement is described in detail, including the unwinding of swap spread trades and decreased foreign willingness to hold Treasuries as contributing factors.
